lines.greek: θῆκε μένος καὶ θάρσος, ὑπέμνησέν τέ ἑ πατρὸς
μᾶλλον ἔτʼ ἢ τὸ πάροιθεν. ὁ δὲ φρεσὶν ᾗσι νοήσας
θάμβησεν κατὰ θυμόν· ὀίσατο γὰρ θεὸν εἶναι.
αὐτίκα δὲ μνηστῆρας ἐπῴχετο ἰσόθεος φώς.
τοῖσι δʼ ἀοιδὸς ἄειδε περικλυτός, οἱ δὲ σιωπῇ
ἥατʼ ἀκούοντες· ὁ δʼ Ἀχαιῶν νόστον ἄειδε
λυγρόν, ὃν ἐκ Τροίης ἐπετείλατο Παλλὰς Ἀθήνη.
τοῦ δʼ ὑπερωιόθεν φρεσὶ σύνθετο θέσπιν ἀοιδὴν
κούρη Ἰκαρίοιο, περίφρων Πηνελόπεια·
κλίμακα δʼ ὑψηλὴν κατεβήσετο οἷο δόμοιο,
οὐκ οἴη, ἅμα τῇ γε καὶ ἀμφίπολοι δύʼ ἕποντο.
ἡ δʼ ὅτε δὴ μνηστῆρας ἀφίκετο δῖα γυναικῶν,
στῆ ῥα παρὰ σταθμὸν τέγεος πύκα ποιητοῖο,
ἄντα παρειάων σχομένη λιπαρὰ κρήδεμνα·
ἀμφίπολος δʼ ἄρα οἱ κεδνὴ ἑκάτερθε παρέστη.
δακρύσασα δʼ ἔπειτα προσηύδα θεῖον ἀοιδόν·
Φήμιε, πολλὰ γὰρ ἄλλα βροτῶν θελκτήρια οἶδας,
ἔργʼ ἀνδρῶν τε θεῶν τε, τά τε κλείουσιν ἀοιδοί·
τῶν ἕν γέ σφιν ἄειδε παρήμενος, οἱ δὲ σιωπῇ
οἶνον πινόντων· ταύτης δʼ ἀποπαύεʼ ἀοιδῆς

interlinear: she-put strength and courage, and-reminded him of-his-father
more still than before. and-he in his-mind perceiving
marvelled in his-heart; for-he-guessed a-god to-be.
and-at-once to-the-wooers he-went, a-man peer-of-the-gods.
and-for-them a-singer sang, a-renowned one; and-they in-silence
sat listening; and-he of-the-Achaeans the-return sang,
the-woeful, which from Troy laid-upon-them Pallas Athena.
and-his, from-the-upper-chamber, in-her-mind marked the-divine song
the-daughter of-Icarius, circumspect Penelope;
and-the-stairway high she-descended of-her house,
not alone, along with-her also handmaids two followed.
and-she, when indeed the-wooers she-reached, bright among-women,
stood then beside the-doorpost of-the-roof strongly built,
before her-cheeks holding her-shining veil;
and-a-handmaid, faithful, on-either-side of-her stood.
and-weeping then she-addressed the-divine singer:
'Phemius, for many other charms of-mortals you-know,
deeds of-men and of-gods, which singers make-famous;
of-these one at-least to-them sing, sitting-by; and-they in-silence
their-wine let-them-drink. but-cease from-this song,
literal: she put strength and courage, and made him think of his father
even more than before. And he perceived it in his mind
and marvelled at heart, for he guessed that she was a god.
At once he went to the wooers, a man peer of the gods.
The renowned singer was singing to them, and they sat
in silence, listening; he sang of the return of the Achaeans,
the woeful return that Pallas Athena laid upon them from Troy.
From her upper chamber the daughter of Icarius,
circumspect Penelope, marked in her mind that divine song,
and she came down the high stairway of her house,
not alone, for two handmaids followed with her.
And when she came to the wooers, bright among women,
she stood beside the doorpost of the strongly built roof,
holding her shining veil before her cheeks;
and a faithful handmaid stood on either side of her.
Then weeping she spoke to the divine singer:
'Phemius, you know many other charms for mortals,
deeds of men and of gods, which singers make famous;
sing them one of these, sitting by, and let them drink
their wine in silence. But cease from this song,
translations.elizabethan:
She left strength in his breast, and courage too,
and set his father in his thought far more
than heretofore. He knew it in his mind
and marvelled, for he guessed she was a god.
Then to the suitors went he, like a god.
The famous singer sang to them; they sat
in silence, listening. Of the Achaeans' voyage
he sang, the bitter homeward road that Pallas
Athena laid upon them out of Troy.
And from the upper room the daughter
of Icarius, circumspect Penelope,
took in that god-taught singing, and came down
the high stair of her house, not by herself,
for two handmaidens followed at her side.
And when she reached the suitors, she who shone
among all women, by the doorpost stood
of the strong-builded roof, and held her veil,
her shining veil, before her cheeks; and each
side of her stood a faithful serving-woman.
Then weeping, to the god-taught man she spoke:
"Phemius, thou knowest many another charm
for mortals, deeds of gods and deeds of men,
which singers make renowned. Sit here and sing
some one of those; and let them drink their wine
in silence. But have done with this thy song."
translations.modernist:
She put strength and courage into him. She made him think about his father, more than before. He worked it out in his mind and it astonished him. He decided she had been a god.

Then he went over to the suitors, a man like a god.

The famous singer was singing to them and they were sitting quiet, listening. He sang about the Achaeans coming back from Troy, and the misery of it, which Pallas Athena had laid on them.

Upstairs the daughter of Icarius heard the singing. Circumspect Penelope. She came down the high staircase of her house. Not alone. Two women came with her.

When she reached the suitors she stood by the doorpost of the great roof, a woman shining among women, and held her bright veil in front of her face. A faithful woman stood on each side of her.

Then she cried, and spoke to the singer.

"Phemius. You know plenty of other things to charm people with. Deeds of men and gods. The things singers make famous. Sit there and sing one of those, and let them drink their wine and say nothing. But stop this song."
translations.storybook:
She left strength and courage behind her in his heart, and made him think about his father even more than before. And when he understood it, he was amazed, for he guessed that his visitor had been a god!

Then he walked over to the suitors, looking like a god himself.

The famous singer was singing for them, and they were all sitting quietly, listening. He was singing about the Achaeans coming home from Troy, and about all the misery that Pallas Athena had piled upon them for it.

Now, up in her room, the daughter of Icarius heard that wonderful song. Wise Penelope came down the tall staircase of her house, and she did not come alone, for two of her women came with her.

When she reached the suitors, shining and lovely as she was, she stood beside the doorpost of the great hall and held her bright veil up in front of her cheeks, with a faithful maid standing on either side of her.

And then she began to cry, and she said to the singer:

"Phemius, you know so many other lovely things to sing! Deeds of gods and deeds of men, all the tales that singers make famous. Sit there and sing one of those instead, and let them drink their wine in silence. But please stop singing this song."

murray.text: For them the famous minstrel was singing, and they sat in silence listening; and he sang of the return of the Achaeans—the woeful return from Troy which Pallas Athena laid upon them. And from her upper chamber the daughter of Icarius, wise Penelope, heard his wondrous song,

and she went down the high stairway from her chamber, not alone, for two handmaids attended her. Now when the fair lady had come to the wooers, she stood by the door-post of the well-built hall, holding before her face her shining veil;

and a faithful handmaid stood on either side of her. Then she burst into tears, and spoke to the divine minstrel: “Phemius, many other things thou knowest to charm mortals, deeds of men and gods which minstrels make famous. Sing them one of these, as thou sittest here,

and let them drink their wine in silence. But cease from this woeful song which ever harrows the heart in my breast, for upon me above all women has come a sorrow not to be forgotten. So dear a head do I ever remember with longing, even my husband, whose fame is wide through Hellas and mid-Argos.”
